label: tetlock-est-Q5
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: estimate ONE sub-question in isolation. You see only the facts and
this sub-question; you must not analyze the broader dispute. Give a calibrated
probability and the few considerations doing the real work.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The Sierra Vista Municipal Water District (invented) serves ~140,000 people in a chronically drought-stressed service area. After a rate study and a majority-protest hearing that drew protests from ~9% of parcels, the board adopted a "Water Resilience Surcharge": $0.85 per hundred cubic feet on residential usage above Tier 2 (the surcharge falls on roughly the top 22% of residential users), projected to raise $4.1M/year into a restricted fund spent as follows: 55% on turf-replacement and high-efficiency-appliance rebates that prioritize low-income customers; 30% on debt service for a recycled-water plant; 15% on "water ambassador" conservation-education staff.

The rate study concludes that high-tier consumption imposes marginal supply costs roughly 1.6× base-tier costs (peaking capacity, drought-reserve purchases), but it derives the $0.85 figure in part from what it calls "conservation-incentive pricing" rather than strictly from cost allocation, and the district concedes the rebate program mostly benefits customers other than those paying the surcharge. A ratepayer association has sued, alleging the surcharge exceeds the proportional cost of service attributable to the payers' parcels in violation of article XIII D, section 6(b) of the California Constitution, citing Capistrano Taxpayers Association v. City of San Juan Capistrano. The district defends the structure under its conservation mandate (Cal. Const. art. X, § 2; Water Code § 375) and argues that, in the alternative, the surcharge is not a property-related fee at all.

## The question

Does the Water Resilience Surcharge violate article XIII D, section 6(b)'s cost-of-service and proportionality requirements? How does Capistrano apply to a tier surcharge that is partly cost-justified (1.6×) and partly incentive-priced, with proceeds partially redistributed as rebates to non-payers? Can article X, section 2's conservation mandate be reconciled with 218, or does 218 win? Is there a colorable argument the surcharge is a tax requiring voter approval (Prop 26), or a non-property-related charge? What is the litigation exposure (refunds, fees) and how could the structure be repaired?

## Sub-question to estimate (in isolation)

**If a court finds some or all of the surcharge violates §6(b), what remedies and exposure follow — full invalidation vs. severance of the 'excess'/incentive-pricing increment, refund liability (measure and look-back period), and attorney-fee shifting — and how does §6(b)(5)'s burden-shifting to the agency affect litigation posture and settlement value?**

What would have to be true: Would need to research: (1) refund mechanics under Gov. Code §§ 53750 et seq. (Prop 218 Omnibus Implementation Act) and any applicable claims-presentation requirements/limitations periods (Gov. Code §§900 et seq., and the ~120-day or statute-specific periods for rate challenges, e.g., Gov. Code §66022/CCP §860 validation-action timing where applicable); (2) whether courts sever only the incentive-priced increment above the 1.6× cost figure and order a partial refund, as opposed to voiding the whole surcharge (look at Capistrano's remedy on remand and Griffith); (3) likelihood of CCP §1021.5 private-attorney-general fee award given public-interest litigation posture and the express burden-shifting in §6(b)(5).
text: {"probability":58,"key_considerations":["Framing: the probability estimates whether, IF a §6(b) violation is found, a court is more likely to order FULL invalidation of the surcharge (voiding the whole $0.85 increment and remanding for a redesigned rate study) rather than a judicially-crafted SEVERANCE that preserves the 1.6x cost-justified portion and refunds only the 'incentive' excess. Courts (per Capistrano's approach — verify precise remedial holding on remand) tend to treat an agency's failure to tie the SET rate to substantiated cost as invalidating the rate as adopted, since rate-repricing is a legislative/administrative function courts are reluctant to perform themselves.","The 55%-to-rebates cross-subsidy (funding benefits mostly to non-payers) is a distinct §6(b)(1)/(2)/(3) defect independent of the incentive-pricing/marginal-cost issue and taints a larger revenue share than just the 'incentive' markup — this pushes toward broader invalidation/larger exposure than a clean severance limited to the incentive-pricing increment, and makes it analytically hard for a court to isolate 'the excess' since two different theories of excess (rebate cross-subsidy vs. incentive pricing above 1.6x) overlap in the same $0.85 figure.","§6(b)(5)'s burden-shifting (agency must affirmatively demonstrate compliance) raises both the odds the district loses outright — its own rate study concedes non-cost 'conservation-incentive pricing' reasoning — and, once liability is found, supports a broader remedy, since the district cannot point to a validly-substantiated dollar figure a court could simply approve as the cost-justified remainder; this materially increases settlement pressure/value independent of which remedy theory ultimately controls.","Refund/look-back exposure is likely narrower than 'full life of the surcharge' — need to verify the district's local claims-presentation ordinance (Gov. Code §§900 et seq./935) and the applicable limitations period (commonly analogized to CCP §338's 3-year period for statutory liability, or a shorter local claims deadline), which would cap dollar exposure well below cumulative collections since adoption; this is a major, currently unverified swing factor on total exposure.","Attorney-fee exposure under CCP §1021.5 is quite likely if plaintiffs prevail on any part of the §6(b) claim — Prop 218 rate challenges are routinely treated as conferring a significant public benefit on a large ratepayer class, and the financial-burden/necessity prong is easily met against a public agency defendant; this fee-shift risk (verify magnitude via Capistrano's post-remand fee award and similar cases such as Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District) substantially raises settlement value even if the refund principal itself is later trimmed by look-back limits or partial severance."],"what_evidence_would_change_this":"Would most shift the estimate: (1) confirming Capistrano's actual disposition on remand/severability — if the Court of Appeal or trial court there in fact upheld a partial refund limited to the unsubstantiated increment (rather than voiding the whole tiered structure), that meaningfully raises the probability of a severed remedy here, especially given this district's more granular 1.6x cost record; (2) the text of the district's rate resolution/rate study — if it discretely itemizes a cost-based sub-rate and a separate incentive add-on (rather than blending them into one $0.85 figure), severance becomes far more administrable and likely; (3) confirmation of the district's local claims ordinance and controlling limitations period, which would let the look-back/refund quantum be estimated with real precision instead of assumed; (4) any case directly addressing whether funding rebates to non-payers from a property-related fee is per se a §6(b)(2)-type violation (versus permissible district-wide conservation benefit) — a clear holding either way would strongly affect whether courts treat the rebate-funded 55% as inherently inseverable from the whole surcharge; (5) reported fee awards in comparable post-Capistrano Prop 218 litigation to calibrate the 1021.5 probability/magnitude more precisely."}

key_considerations: Framing: the probability estimates whether, IF a §6(b) violation is found, a court is more likely to order FULL invalidation of the surcharge (voiding the whole $0.85 increment and remanding for a redesigned rate study) rather than a judicially-crafted SEVERANCE that preserves the 1.6x cost-justified portion and refunds only the 'incentive' excess. Courts (per Capistrano's approach — verify precise remedial holding on remand) tend to treat an agency's failure to tie the SET rate to substantiated cost as invalidating the rate as adopted, since rate-repricing is a legislative/administrative function courts are reluctant to perform themselves.

The 55%-to-rebates cross-subsidy (funding benefits mostly to non-payers) is a distinct §6(b)(1)/(2)/(3) defect independent of the incentive-pricing/marginal-cost issue and taints a larger revenue share than just the 'incentive' markup — this pushes toward broader invalidation/larger exposure than a clean severance limited to the incentive-pricing increment, and makes it analytically hard for a court to isolate 'the excess' since two different theories of excess (rebate cross-subsidy vs. incentive pricing above 1.6x) overlap in the same $0.85 figure.

§6(b)(5)'s burden-shifting (agency must affirmatively demonstrate compliance) raises both the odds the district loses outright — its own rate study concedes non-cost 'conservation-incentive pricing' reasoning — and, once liability is found, supports a broader remedy, since the district cannot point to a validly-substantiated dollar figure a court could simply approve as the cost-justified remainder; this materially increases settlement pressure/value independent of which remedy theory ultimately controls.

Refund/look-back exposure is likely narrower than 'full life of the surcharge' — need to verify the district's local claims-presentation ordinance (Gov. Code §§900 et seq./935) and the applicable limitations period (commonly analogized to CCP §338's 3-year period for statutory liability, or a shorter local claims deadline), which would cap dollar exposure well below cumulative collections since adoption; this is a major, currently unverified swing factor on total exposure.

Attorney-fee exposure under CCP §1021.5 is quite likely if plaintiffs prevail on any part of the §6(b) claim — Prop 218 rate challenges are routinely treated as conferring a significant public benefit on a large ratepayer class, and the financial-burden/necessity prong is easily met against a public agency defendant; this fee-shift risk (verify magnitude via Capistrano's post-remand fee award and similar cases such as Plantier v. Ramona Municipal Water District) substantially raises settlement value even if the refund principal itself is later trimmed by look-back limits or partial severance.
data.what_evidence_would_change_this: Would most shift the estimate: (1) confirming Capistrano's actual disposition on remand/severability — if the Court of Appeal or trial court there in fact upheld a partial refund limited to the unsubstantiated increment (rather than voiding the whole tiered structure), that meaningfully raises the probability of a severed remedy here, especially given this district's more granular 1.6x cost record; (2) the text of the district's rate resolution/rate study — if it discretely itemizes a cost-based sub-rate and a separate incentive add-on (rather than blending them into one $0.85 figure), severance becomes far more administrable and likely; (3) confirmation of the district's local claims ordinance and controlling limitations period, which would let the look-back/refund quantum be estimated with real precision instead of assumed; (4) any case directly addressing whether funding rebates to non-payers from a property-related fee is per se a §6(b)(2)-type violation (versus permissible district-wide conservation benefit) — a clear holding either way would strongly affect whether courts treat the rebate-funded 55% as inherently inseverable from the whole surcharge; (5) reported fee awards in comparable post-Capistrano Prop 218 litigation to calibrate the 1021.5 probability/magnitude more precisely.
